topic: A person learns their apartment building is going condo
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mailman dropped off a thick, glossy brochure this morning, and I stared at it until the edges blurred. It detailed a proposed transition: my familiar, slightly worn apartment building, the one I’d called home for seven years, was slated to become a luxury condominium complex. My hands, usually steady when I was organizing my spice rack, were trembling slightly as I reread the architectural renderings. What was happening? The familiar scent of old coffee and dust in the hallway suddenly felt heavy, suffocating. I walked to the window, peering down at the street, half-expecting to see a construction crew already staking out the perimeter. A low, hollow sound escaped my throat. I picked up my phone, my thumb hovering over my contact list, unable to articulate the sheer strangeness of the news settling in my chest.

[story 2]

"So, they're turning it into condos?" I asked Sarah over lukewarm takeout, the sound of the city traffic outside seeming distant and irrelevant. I took a long sip of my soda, the fizz doing little to settle the knot tightening in my stomach. I kept glancing at the news feed on my tablet, rereading the developer's press release for the tenth time. It made no sense. This building had been my sanctuary; the peeling paint on the stairwell, the specific creak in the floorboards in the living room—all of it was suddenly being appraised, repackaged, sold off. I pushed my plate away, the clatter echoing too loudly in the small kitchen. I started pacing the length of the room, my movements jerky, as if trying to physically shake the unbelievable reality out of my system.

[story 3]

The notice was taped squarely to the elevator bank, stark white against the institutional grey. I read the fine print, my eyes skipping over the jargon until they landed on the final sentence: 'Phase one conversion to high-end residential units commencing Q3.' I stood there, rooted to the spot, feeling a strange disconnect between the words on the paper and the tangible reality of my life unfolding around me. I traced the outline of the letter with my finger, the paper surprisingly smooth under my touch. A slow, almost involuntary sigh escaped me. I turned away from the notice, walking toward the front door, but my feet felt anchored to the linoleum floor. Every familiar corner of the hallway seemed to shift, rearranging itself into something entirely foreign and unsettling.
